title: Oracle Quietly Halves Free Tier Ampere A1 Compute Limits with No Public Announcement
gist: Oracle halved its Always Free Ampere A1 allowance (4 OCPU/24GB to 2 OCPU/12GB) effective June 15 2026 with no blog post, email, or clear documentation, eroding developer trust; ambiguity over whether PAYG accounts are affected and a fine-print termination trap compound the reputational damage.

insights.claim: Oracle reduced its Always Free Ampere A1 Compute allowance on OCI from 4 OCPUs/24GB RAM (3,000 OCPU hours, 18,000 GB hours/month) to 2 OCPUs/12GB (1,500 OCPU hours, 9,000 GB hours), effective June 15, 2026, with no blog post, customer notification, or public announcement.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A material silent cut to a flagship free tier — a concrete signal of Oracle tightening free-cloud economics.
insights.locator: Oracle has reduced the Always Free Ampere A1 Compute allowance

insights.claim: The previously generous 4 OCPU/24GB free tier attracted a large community of developers, self-hosters, and Linux enthusiasts running personal servers, test environments, lightweight AI inference with Ollama, and home automation.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: The free tier was a developer-acquisition funnel; halving it risks the grassroots adoption that seeds enterprise pipelines.
insights.locator: The previous allowance of 3,000 OCPU hours

insights.claim: Oracle human support agents (not the chatbot) confirmed via email on June 22 that the new limits apply only to free-tier accounts and PAYG accounts may still use 4 OCPUs/24GB free, but Oracle issued no public clarification and its documentation still says 'all tenancies' get only the reduced limits.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Users relying on support-email screenshots rather than policy face billing uncertainty — a governance red flag for platform reliance.
insights.quote: If PAYG tenancies are truly unaffected, as Oracle Support is reportedly telling customers, then this wording is very misleading.

insights.claim: Grandfathered 4/24 instances are one termination away from being permanently locked to the new 2/12 limits, because Oracle's fine print states terminated resources above the updated limit may not be recreatable.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Any maintenance event, outage, or user mistake permanently downgrades capacity — a hidden operational risk for anyone building on the grandfathered tier.
insights.quote: If an existing resource is terminated, it may not be possible to recreate resources above the updated Always Free limit.
insights.locator: Another commenter flagged the fine print

themes.theme: Trust erosion as the real story

insights.claim: Halving a free tier is a legitimate business decision, but doing it without a blog post, email, or clear documentation distinguishing free-tier from PAYG impact creates trust erosion that makes developers hesitant to build on a platform.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: The reputational cost of opaque communication, not the cut itself, is the durable risk to Oracle's developer-platform positioning.
insights.quote: The community found out through documentation diffs and broken instances, not through Oracle.
insights.locator: The way Oracle handled this change is the real story

insights.claim: Every cloud provider adjusts free offerings over time, with AWS having introduced a new free plan the prior year, framing Oracle's move as industry-normal in substance but abnormal in execution.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Context that free-tier tightening is a broad industry trend — watch for further contraction of generous cloud free tiers.
insights.locator: Every cloud provider adjusts free offerings over time
